questions.text: What does the Program Counter (PC) hold?

options.text: The result of the last calculation
The memory address of the NEXT instruction to fetch
The number of instructions executed so far

questions.explanation: The PC always points to the next instruction to be fetched. After each fetch, the PC is incremented so it points to the instruction after that.

questions.text: In which stage of the fetch-decode-execute cycle is the PC incremented?
questions.explanation: The PC is incremented during the fetch stage, after the current instruction's address has been sent to the MAR. This prepares it to point to the next instruction.

questions.text: What is the key principle of Von Neumann architecture?

options.text: Instructions and data are stored in separate memories
Instructions and data are stored in the same memory
The CPU can only process one instruction at a time
All data must pass through the GPU before processing

questions.explanation: Von Neumann architecture's defining feature is a single shared memory for both program instructions and data. This differs from Harvard architecture which separates them.

questions.text: Which component of the CPU performs arithmetic and logical operations?
questions.explanation: The ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit) handles all arithmetic operations (+, -, ×, ÷) and logical operations (AND, OR, NOT, comparisons).

questions.text: A computer has 4 GHz clock speed. How many clock cycles does it perform per second?
questions.explanation: 1 GHz = 1 billion (10⁹) cycles per second. 4 GHz = 4,000,000,000 cycles per second.

questions.text: Which type of primary storage is volatile?
questions.explanation: RAM is volatile — it loses all data when power is cut. ROM, flash memory, and SSDs are all non-volatile.

options.text: A very fast type of memory built into the CPU
A section of secondary storage used as an extension of RAM
Memory used by the GPU for graphics rendering

questions.explanation: When RAM is full, the OS uses part of the hard drive or SSD as virtual memory. It is slower than RAM but prevents the system from running out of memory entirely.

options.text: The memory address of the location being read from or written to
The result of the last ALU operation
The data that was just fetched from memory

questions.explanation: The MAR (Memory Address Register) holds the address of the memory location being accessed. The actual data is held in the MDR.

questions.text: Which secondary storage type has NO moving parts?
questions.explanation: An SSD (Solid State Drive) stores data in flash memory chips with no moving parts, making it faster, more durable, and quieter than HDDs and optical drives.

questions.text: Which bus carries data in both directions between the CPU and memory?
questions.explanation: The data bus is bidirectional — it carries data and instructions between the CPU and memory in both directions. The address bus is one-way (CPU → memory).

questions.text: What is the role of the Control Unit in the CPU?

options.text: It stores the result of calculations
It coordinates all CPU activities and sends control signals

questions.explanation: The Control Unit (CU) coordinates all CPU activities. It decodes instructions and sends control signals to components (ALU, registers, memory) to carry them out.

questions.text: Adding more cache memory to a CPU will most likely:

options.text: Reduce the number of slow RAM accesses and improve performance

questions.explanation: Cache stores frequently used data extremely close to the CPU. More cache means the CPU can retrieve data quickly without accessing slower RAM, improving overall performance.
